reasoning: The save() function in save.php lines 19-20 requires both ADS_TXT_MANAGE_CAPABILITY (edit_ads_txt, administrator-only) and a valid nonce via check_admin_referer('adstxt_save'). Line 24 casts $_post['post_id'] to int and uses it as 'ID' in wp_insert_post at line 89 without verifying the caller owns that specific post. However, the capability required is administrator-level. WordPress administrators already have edit_others_posts and delete_others_posts capabilities, meaning they can mutate any post through standard WordPress admin interfaces. There is no privilege escalation, no cross-role boundary crossing, and no access to data or actions the attacker could not already perform. The WordPress security model explicitly treats administrator-to-administrator attacks as out of scope. The finding describes a plugin-internal logic inconsistency (the plugin should only mutate its own adstxt posts, not arbitrary posts) but not an exploitable vulnerability that grants capabilities beyond what the attacker already possesses. This is a false positive under the standard WordPress broken access control threat model.
